sentences.text: Yes, Henry H.
Oster.
Yes, O-S-T-E-R.
November 5, 1928.
67, this year.
I was born in Cologne, Koln, Germany.
Yes.
His name was Hans Isadore Oster.
My mother's name was Lisbeth Oster.
Certainly can.

Having been born in Germany in 1928, obviously pre-Hitler days, little did I, of course, understand or know anything before the age approximately six, from what I understood, it was a reasonably normal, somewhat average life for a middle class Jewish family.
The first recollection that I had was the times I spent with my parents and grandparents, both of whom lived in different cities.
And as a little child, it was just like anywhere else, a happy-go-lucky time.
The first incident and awareness of changing times was when I was approximately six years old, which by then, of course, was 1934.
Hitler had come to power.
And you sensed, as a child, disturbances in the family, restlessness, maybe somewhat confusion.
But as a six-year-old, you're not really aware what normal times are.
The first recollection that I had as to anti-Semitism or fascism affecting my life was the very first day I had to go to school, which, of course, was in 1934.
Hitler had been in power now for a year and some months.
And the only thing that I thought was a wonderful day, anticipation, the first day in school, as an any child would.
And leaving the school, we were greeted with stones and rocks and unpleasant comments.
A couple of spitting incidents--
This was still, at that time, a Jewish grade school, you might say.
It was mixed with very, very few Gentiles.
But it was a Jewish school predominantly because it had Jewish teachers and predominantly Jewish students.
The incident was sort of inconsequential, I thought, because children being children, you felt, well, you know, there's nothing major significance to this kind of incident.
My parents, I know, were very disturbed, and they were very unhappy that the first day would be so quickly ruined with unpleasantness.
I went to school from 1934 to about 1936, to the same school.

A lot things have changed, of course, in Germany in those intervening years, especially with the announcement of the Nuremberg Laws, where all the civil rights and all the privileges had been taken away from Jews.
And our life became very constrained, meaning that it was very obvious, the things you could, or most important, could not do.
You could not attend any public facilities, whether it be sports stadium, movies, opera, concert.
Everything was being strictly prohibited by signs appearing on the buildings, on the theater, saying Jews are prohibited from entering.
My own personal experience was, I had seen, until I was well past the age of 18, one single, solitary movie, which was the last chance my parents had to take me.
And it turned out to be-- I've forgotten the name, but a Shirley Temple movie.
And that was sort of a very, you might say, significant and obvious restraint that I had to put up with because children like to go to movies.
And here, one of those pleasant things that each child is looking forward to could not be attended by me or my parents.
Schooling itself became now very erratic.
Many things happened, which I won't necessarily go into all these details.

But the school was the obvious activities for a child, and as a result, you almost had this little pleasure that, hmm, I don't have to go to school every day, although you quickly realized that it wasn't the wishful thinking that came true.
It was just the legal prohibition of Jews attending their regular schools.
The school itself was closed in, I believe, 1936.
And you were, from then on, sort of haphazardly attending classes that were clandestine, the teachers would hold in their homes, or perhaps in a larger facility.
We did still have some Jewish homes available of size large enough to attend, let's say, 10, 15 students, but the formal schooling had stopped, for me at least, in 1936.
From 1936 to 1938, family life was totally disrupted because my father was forced to attend a-- not a concentration camp, but a forced labor camp.
My father was the vice-president of a chain of stores that-- that sold white goods, dresses, perfumes, soaps, and so on.
He was employed by a Jewish company, and-- which of course, was taken over by Nazi party members. and my father had not worked since 1936 with any regularity.
The business was not closed but prohibited to be owned by any Jews.
And in 1937, he was forced to-- he was forced into forced labor.
The work he had to do was build the Maginot Line in France.
The Germans on their side called it the Siegfried Line.
The Siegfried Line was a fortification along the French border to prevent tanks from crossing borders either way.
Obviously, my father being German, he had to build the Siegfried Line.
That was the first, you know, major disruption of family life.
I stayed home with my mother, who never worked.
She was a housewife.
And we subsisted on the very, very meager pay that my father was sent.
He was paid, but forced labor is just not a very [LAUGHS] well-paid occupation.
The other thing that became very noticeable was that we were not allowed to have any basic outside communication.
We were not allowed to own radios.
We were not allowed to have subscription to newspapers or magazines.
You basically sat around the house waiting for who knows what to happen.
In 1938, you also noticed a very substantial upheaval in the population in general.

This was long before the November event, which I'll come to in a moment, because what Germany was doing at that time, including the evidence that my father had to work at the Siegfried Line, was the preparation for World War II.
This kind of came right down to the population level that many things were happening more dramatically, more rapidly, and you might say, even more significantly.
The most important thing, of course, that everybody in Germany wore uniforms, uniforms, uniforms.
Everybody--
Oh, yes, because first of all, you were not readily going out on the street because you didn't wear a Hitler Youth uniform.
And you were, of course, later on asked and forced to wear the-- the yellow star.
But even when you didn't wear a uniform as a child, you were suspected of being non-Aryan, obviously Jewish.
And children would set upon you, and with their little knives that they had, threaten you, kick you, beat you.
And you were basically defenseless because if you involved yourself in the brawl, adults would come very quickly and beatings and abuses would get even worse.
And this, of course, became increasingly restricted.
You became a hermit-like living style, and consequently, with a great deal of depression on the family members.
In this case, my mother and I lived alone, which was in a very nice street, which we were forced to vacate because the apartment house was owned by Gentiles.

The force to move came, of course, in 1938, with the Crystal Night, or the Night of Crystal, Kristallnacht, which happened on the 9th of November, at which date, you know, all hell broke loose all over Germany.

I realize the Germans always claim that it was coincidental, but in every major city, it was a fully organized and planned signal that from now on, you know, persecution of the Jews will have to be to a most dramatic and not yet final solution, but dramatic end.
Very much so.
You don't forget these things from first of all, purely visual point of view.
We lived on a street, where as it is very common in Europe, that you have large apartment houses, where you live above the first floor, the ground floor, the ground floor being mostly stores.
We have areas in the United States that are very similar, especially San Francisco.
There are whole districts where you have small stores, small businesses.
Maybe you might call them, in those days, mom and pop stores, and you lived above that.
That's a very common European architectural style.

When you live in such a street, in such an area, and you hear incredible noises, which means the shattering of glass, looting, yelling, screaming, and most important of all, the order of fires, of smoke, when stores and business were set aflame, and of course, all the synagogues.
We happened to have lived in a fairly close proximity to the major synagogue.
And if you had gone on the street, which was not safe to do, we could've actually seen the synagogue burning.
My particular synagogue, yes.
People did tend to stay close to their own temples or synagogues because it wasn't customary in those days to own cars and automobiles, like we know in the United States.
We were Reform Jews.
We kept a very strict household, not kosher, but religious holidays, and we went to temple on Friday nights, sometimes on Saturday mornings.
Obviously, at that time, I was not 13 years old.
But I had anticipated my bar mitzvah sometime down the road.
And so it became a very comfortable and I would say routine activity for us to attend Friday night synagogue, yes.
And that was the synagogue, the largest in Cologne.
They had some smaller ones, but this was a very, very major structure.
And obviously, all of the synagogues in major cities had been destroyed that particular evening.
Well, my-- I only had one grandmother on my father's side.
She lived in Bad Godesberg.
Bad Godesberg, unfortunately, was Hitler's favorite retreat for a vacation.
And my family had lived there for over 180 years.
And they were a very prominent family, having grown up for such a long time in one community.
My other parents were-- well, he was a little winemaker, as we would say, and he lived in Bad Kreuznach.
Now interestingly enough, cities with a B-A-D in front of it means it's usually a resort, a recreational city, built up for various reasons, baths and so on.
And so they were very popular cities in which it was actually quite favorable for Jews to live and to thrive before 1933.
But that is the-- the family background on my grandparents' side.
Yes, up to 1936, you were still able to use public transportation with some degree of safety because you weren't recognized to be Jewish.
Before we had to wear the star, there was no distinction.
And it was rather on a regular basis, once or twice a year, to go and visit my grandparents, or my grandmother, in one case, yes.

The problems of 1938, of course, caused a rapid worsening of Jewish life in-- in Germany, especially in Cologne, Cologne having been a interesting city culturally and many other ways, and also strategically located near the French border.
It's certainly very close to it.

And also, it was a city that ultimately, with respect to World War II, became more strategic because it was a railroad facility that led into the countries that had been invaded, like Belgium and Holland.
In 1938, it became totally impossible to have any kind of a normal life.
Of course, for a 10-year-old that was puzzling. and you were quite confused as to the events to come.
All you know, you were terribly intimidated.
Now, you really didn't leave the house.
And most important of all, the house we lived in, we had to vacate, which was actually an apartment, I should say.
We happened to be fortunate enough that my parents and I found a-- one room, with a tiny kitchen, in a Jewish-owned house, apartment house, which we did share with Gentiles.
Now, because it was a free-for-all against the Jews since 1938, the Gentiles in that house were not too kind to any coexistence with Jews in the same-- it was absolutely unpatriotic.
It was-- you know, you were an absolute enemy.

Of course, they did know they were-- that we were Jewish. and we knew that they were Gentiles because when they came up and down the stairs in their little uniforms, as the kids, boys and girls, or the adults having joined the party, it was incredibly intimidating.
You barely stuck your head out-- out of the-- the room you lived in, which is, interestingly enough, a peculiar situation as we know the United States.
Why?
Because we had to share bathrooms.
German apartments did not have private bathrooms.
Usually, when you lived in an apartment house, you had a landing, and the family from above and the family from below had to use the facilities on that landing.
And you actually had to wait till it was absolutely safe even to use the bathroom.
The question-- the question that came up, of course, most often is what can you expect from your own neighbors?
Absolutely nothing.
You couldn't ask, and you wouldn't receive a copy of a newspaper.
You had no radio.
You were cut off from the world.
And the only way that you could possibly get some information was a fairly common European, as well as a German, accommodation that newspapers were pasted on a wall.

And anybody who could not afford or who had no access to the newspaper, and if you didn't have the star at that time, you could go to the corner and read the daily newspaper to know what was going on, but you did it pretty fast because you just simply didn't spend much time outdoors.
1938-- it was then, also, the total elimination of any-- any trace of personal freedom.
There was just absolutely nothing.
My father still had not returned from his forced labor camp, and my mother and I were simply wiling away the time waiting for, I guess, history to happen.
Of course, it didn't take very long that we had World War II in September of 1939, at which time, things became even more panicky.
Jews were now gradually rounded up to places unknown.

The Crystal Night, or the Kristallnacht, did produce the first arrest of over 30,000 men, who did disappear, [INTERPOSING VOICES] only to return later on to find out that they had gone, in Germany, to build certain camps, certain facilities.
So we did have those people that returned tell us that certain things were being built but that had not yet been utilized to the extreme measure that occurred later on.
Well, obviously, looking back, you understand many more things than you did at the time.
You were obviously intimidated.
You lived in fear of the unknown.
You were definitely exposed to the cruelty of the-- the population, especially children of your own age.
Everybody felt that they were patriotic to hate, to beat, to report Jews for not even the slightest infraction, which you were careful not to do.
You were arrested for anything whatsoever.
If you were suspected to have a kind of clandestine radio, or if they did find in your mailbox when you hardly received mail, a newspaper, it was cause for an arrest.
Of course, my father was in a forced labor camp.
So he would-- they always, of course, chose the male of the household.
Well, since I was the male of the household, so to speak, you assumed certain responsibilities with your little understanding that you had for a 10-year-old.

But you were always concerned about anything that you saw or heard or did to be an infraction of the rules, or as far as the Germans are concerned, breaking the law with really very serious consequences, including disappearance and not returning.
No, you don't have to be told.
You were fearful on your own.
You were fearful of your own experiences.
You stepped out of the-- the house, you looked around, never knowing what you could expect.
There was a sort of patriotic responsibility to beat up on Jews.
The little kids were heroes to the family, to the Nazi organization.
It was an indoctrination that anything you do against the Jews was not only OK, but it was a privilege and you became a little hero.
And you would-- sometimes would have kids wait at the door, trying to beat you up to be able to go back and say, look I beat up a Jewish boy or little Jewish girl.
Yes, but they were not of great, serious consequences.
I-- I was not hurt.
But you were constantly shoved.

Now, one of the things that was really the most, it seems strange, dangerous is to go for the few privileges you had to the grocery store, where obviously, you had to buy your food with the meager earnings that my father provided.
And you were intimidated and looked upon, spat upon.
And very often, if they knew you were Jewish, you simply weren't served.

And believe me, people talk, and people let it be known, you know, that little Henry-- or Heinz, as my name was, you know-- that's that little Jewish boy from Blumenstrasse at 15, which actually, that simply said they knew your address.

Many, many Jews, of course, had managed to leave Germany, and that simply meant those of us who were left were really the recipients of all the abuse and hatred the Germans had to vent, the leftovers.
And we were quite spread out throughout the city.
And so consequently, you had maybe one or two families in the neighborhood.
And hey, it was open game.
You know, get that Jew, and whatever you can do, you're the big shot for it.
So it was a very, you might say, peculiar awareness of how the world around you was crumbling and was crumbling in on you.
And this was, up to 1939, a very common and, of course, very uniform kind of disposition that, at least, children of my age had to put up with.
Did you ever see your father in this time?
He was allowed to come home on weekends.
I believe, if I'm not mistaken, one weekend a month in about four years, three-and-a-half, four years-- one weekend a month, and not on a regular basis, yes.
They gave you a furlough.
And this was, of course, in 1939 that he finally came home before the war started because the work he was forced to do, it was-- had been completed anticipating the 1939 war with-- with France.
After 1939, when the war had started, it became of course, even more incredible to live in Germany.
And the main reason for that, we now had-- to all the burdens we had to suffer, air raids.
Allied bombing took Cologne as a huge and major target because it had railroad facilities leading into all these now occupied countries.

And the idea was that if you bombed German cities, why not the target that currently would be the most critical to cut off supplies to the occupied countries that Germany had occupied with World War II, including France?
And we suffered every day and every night.
Cologne was the first city to have daylight and nighttime bombing.
What made it so incredible for Jews is that we were not allowed to seek shelter.
We were not permitted to public shelters if you should happen to be on the street and an air raid was going on, nor could you go into the basement air shelter of your own apartment house.
They did, of course, and sometimes, because of panic, let us in because we panicked ourselves.
But I would say 90% of the time, we had to stay in our one-room little place.
And we did get bombed, not directly, but surrounding, which was very, very frightening.
And one time, we-- I woke up having been-- having fallen out of bed.
I must say, what we call bed-- this wasn't a bed.
This was just a little combination of slats upon which we put a pillow and a-- a blanket.
And that was in the foot of the bed that my parents slept, also not quite a bed, but I mean, a lot closer.

And so the first time that we were sort of forced out of the bed by a very near hit, we tried to get in the air raid-- basement air raid shelter, and the people in the apartment house wouldn't even open the door.
So we returned back to our room.
But obviously, as you can see, we survived.

Now, those were the times in 1939, with a further deterioration of course, a life that we had in Germany, because now the Germans considered the Jews not only being Jews or enemies of the country, now, they labeled the Jews as traitors.
We obviously have to be identified.
So consequently, we had to wear a star with the word 'Jew' on it, 'Jude' of course, in German, on our left front breast.
Yes- you-- Yes, the way they were issued was we did have sort of a, supposedly, a-- a Jewish public service building, where a few organizational activities took place.
You couldn't congregate, of course.
The main reason, as I found out many, many years later, was that the main responsibility for this building to be permitted to exist was to consistently have an update where the Jews were living.
